problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem A (continuous): model the spatial-temporal temperature of bathwater in a non-circulating tub that cools by losing heat to air and tub, and find the best strategy for a constant trickle of hot water from the faucet to keep the bath near-uniform and near-constant in temperature without wasting water. This team (#48649) splits it into a bathtub-without-person (BWOP) spatial-distribution simulation and a bathtub-with-person (BWP) lumped natural-cooling + inflow-strategy model, then chooses between continuous and discontinuous refill strategies via a multi-criteria fuzzy evaluation.
models_used: BWOP (Bathtub Without Person) model: 3-D steady CFD/heat-transfer simulation of the temperature field, solved with the Finite Element Method in ANSYS FLUENT
Hot-water tube simplification: the faucet-to-overflow heating is idealized as a hot-water heating tube ('tube heater') so heat addition is analytically tractable before the full ANSYS run

Natural-convection governing PDE system: continuity, 3-D Navier-Stokes momentum equations (with Boussinesq buoyancy term), and the convection-diffusion energy equation, with a steady-state assumption (∂t/∂τ = 0) and 3rd-kind (Robin) boundary condition at the water surface

Empirical heat-transfer-coefficient correlations: free-convection Nu = 0.54 Ra^(1/4) (with Gr, Ra, Pr) for the water-air surface; Gnielinski-type internal forced-convection correlation Nu = (f/8)RePr / [1.07 + 12.7(f/8)^0.5(Pr^(2/3)-1)] with friction factor f = (0.79 ln Re - 1.64)^-2 for the tube; cylindrical conduction resistance ln(r_out/r_in) for the tube wall

BWP (Bathtub With Person) lumped/homogeneous-temperature model: water treated as a single uniform-temperature body, energy balance giving a natural-cooling ODE solved numerically in MATLAB; heat loss split into Water-Air convection (Q1) and a series Water-Bathtub conduction + Bathtub-Air convection path (Q2)
Continuous Inflow (CI) strategy model: steady energy balance Q_in·Δτ = Q_d·Δτ + cρV·ΔT with constant faucet flow tuned so heat-in = heat-loss to hold ~313 K
Discontinuous Inflow (DCI) strategy model: bang-bang / thermostat (hysteresis) control with low threshold t_l = 312 K and high threshold t_h = 314 K, alternating heating and natural-cooling stages

Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation (FCE): multi-criteria decision model that builds a fuzzy relation matrix R from relative deviations to an ideal point, derives criterion weights from coefficient-of-variation (w_j = V_j/ΣV_j), and ranks strategies by F = R·ω^T

CBBA (Cleansing with Bubble Bath Additive) extension: bubble layer modeled as a porous-medium conduction layer between water and air with its own conductivity λ_bub, added as an extra thermal-insulation resistance
key_techniques: Two-regime decomposition: a high-fidelity 3-D FEM/CFD field model (no person, to expose spatial gradients) plus a reduced lumped ODE model (with person, justified because a bather agitates the water to uniformity) — using the right tool for each sub-question instead of forcing one model

Analytical simplification before simulation: replacing the real faucet-jet inflow with an equivalent 'hot-water tube' so heat-transfer coefficients can be hand-derived, then validating/refining with the real geometry in ANSYS FLUENT

Quantitative comfort and safety metrics extracted from the field: volume-average (40.4 C), volume-max (318 K) and volume-min temperatures, and the argument that the 45 C hot spot near the faucet stays well below the 55 C scald limit — tying the model back to human-relevant criteria

Defining soft, qualitative requirements as concrete formulas: comfort = 1/std-dev of temperature (Cd), operation complexity = 1/operation-period (Oc), plus an operative-difficulty index η — turning 'comfortable' and 'low effort' into computable numbers
Data-driven weighting in FCE via coefficient of variation (V_j = S_j/mean), so the criterion weights ω = (0.3, 0.15, 0.15, 0.4) come from the data spread rather than arbitrary judge-pleasing guesses

Control-variate sensitivity sweeps over inflow velocity, inflow temperature, air temperature (BWOP) and tub surface area, volume, material conductivity, and bather volume (BWP), each with tables + trend graphs, used to identify water volume as the dominant driver and to show the system is robust
Physically-grounded ranking of effects: showing tub-wall heat loss is <10% of water-air convection, which justifies the adiabatic-wall assumption and the later neglect of tub material/surface area
Communication-ready appendix: a plain-language 'make your own spa tub' user letter translating Strategy 1 (CI) and Strategy 2 (DCI) into actionable consumer instructions with scald warnings
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness and the marriage of real engineering simulation with decision analysis. It does what the problem literally asks — temperature in BOTH space and time — by running an actual 3-D ANSYS FLUENT FEM simulation that produces isothermal field plots, a meshed geometry, and a volume-integral report (avg/max/min temp, wall vs surface heat flux), which reads as genuine, verifiable work rather than hand-waving. It then answers the 'best strategy' half rigorously by formalizing four competing criteria (water used, comfort, operation complexity, difficulty) and resolving the trade-off with Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation, ending in a clean numeric verdict (F_DCI = 0.14 < F_CI = 0.36, so discontinuous refill wins). The sensitivity analysis is broad and the conclusions are physically sane and self-consistent (water volume dominates; tub material/area negligible because wall loss <10% of surface loss), which signals the team understands the physics, not just the software. It also handles every sub-question including the bubble-additive extension, and closes with a consumer-facing letter — the kind of polish and full-scope coverage MCM judges reward."
section_highlights: "Summary + Abstract clearly name the model split (BWOP via FEM/ANSYS, BWP via MATLAB thermodynamic solving) and preview the FCE decision step, so a judge knows the whole arc in one page. Assumptions are individually justified with a one-line physical rationale (e.g., bather agitation justifies uniform temperature; air treated as infinite reservoir), and crucially the Weaknesses section revisits assumptions 3, 4, 5 by name and states exactly how each erodes precision — honest and specific. A clean Notations table separates constants (with cited values/units) from variables. Two framework flowcharts (Graph 2 and Graph 8) give the model architecture at a glance. The BWOP results section couples isothermal diagrams with a volume-integral data table and then interprets them in human terms (40 C average, localized 45 C hot spot below 55 C scald limit). The sensitivity section is consistently formatted: parameter table → trend graph → physical interpretation. The strengths/weaknesses section is concrete (FEM gives a real distribution, lumped simplification is justified by motility, discrete strategy is more operable) rather than boilerplate."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Steady-state assumption is in tension with the problem: BWOP solves a steady temperature field (∂t/∂τ = 0), so it shows the spatial distribution but does not actually model time evolution of the field — the time dimension is handled only in the separate lumped BWP model, so space and time are never modeled together in one solver

The uniform-temperature BWP assumption is a strong simplification justified only by 'the bather will stir the water'; the paper itself admits (assumptions 4 and 5) this converts convection into conduction and reduces accuracy, and it sidesteps the genuinely hard moving-fluid-plus-body problem the prompt implies
Tub walls assumed adiabatic in BWOP but conducting in BWP — an internal inconsistency the team papers over by noting wall loss is small (<10%), rather than reconciling the two treatments
Heat exchange between the water and the person's body is explicitly ignored, even though a 37 C body in 40 C water is a real heat path and the bather is the whole point of the problem

FCE has weakly-justified, partly arbitrary inputs: the operative-difficulty indices (η = 10 for CI, 1 for DCI) are hand-set, and the comfort metric 1/std-dev plus a small sample of points makes the weights and the final F ranking somewhat fragile

Several sensitivity curves (e.g., surface area, material) are essentially identical across cases — the model is so dominated by water-air convection that those sweeps confirm robustness but add little discriminating insight, and some plotted/derived numbers (e.g., one heatmap value 3.14e+01, inflow velocity units switching m/s vs m/min) show transcription/units sloppiness

Equation (4.9) for h_water is garbled/likely mis-typeset, and key parameters (tube radius, length, emissivity, bubble-layer thickness) are introduced but never given concrete values, so parts of the heat-addition derivation are not fully reproducible
CBBA bubble model is only a thin conduction-layer add-on with assumed λ_bub values and no calibration to real foam properties — illustrative rather than validated
reusable_patterns: Split a hard coupled problem into a high-fidelity field model for one sub-question and a reduced lumped model for another, each with an explicit justification for why the simplification is acceptable in that regime

Use professional simulation software (ANSYS FLUENT / FEM) to produce concrete, visual, verifiable artifacts — meshed geometry, isothermal plots, and a volume-integral data table — which reads as real engineering and impresses judges

Pre-simplify with an analytic surrogate (the 'hot-water tube' equivalent) to derive heat-transfer coefficients by hand, then run the true geometry numerically — analytics for insight, simulation for accuracy
Turn vague qualitative requirements (comfort, low effort) into explicit computable metrics: comfort = 1/std-dev, complexity = 1/period, etc.

Resolve multi-criteria strategy choice with Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation, deriving weights from the data's coefficient of variation rather than guessing, and report a single scalar score for a crisp recommendation
Model an on/off refill policy as thermostat/hysteresis (bang-bang) control with explicit high/low thresholds, and compare it head-to-head against a continuous-control alternative
Run control-variate sensitivity sweeps as parameter-table → trend-graph → physical-interpretation triples, and use them to rank which inputs actually matter (here: water volume dominates)
Justify dropping a term by quantifying its relative magnitude (wall loss <10% of surface loss) instead of asserting it
Add new physics as an extra thermal-resistance layer (bubble layer as porous-medium conduction) to extend a base model to a new scenario cheaply
Close with a plain-language, audience-specific deliverable (the 'make your own spa tub' user letter) that translates the math into actionable advice — strong for the communication score
